description:
You are acting as a junior backup-operator who has to prove that a tiny user-space backup script can be triggered both by classic cron **and** by a user-level systemd timer.  
When you finish, automated tests will verify:

1. the existence and executability of one shell script;  
2. the correct placement and contents of one cron specification file;  
3. the correct placement and contents of one systemd service **and** timer unit;  
4. the fact that the backup script was executed at least once and produced a line in a log file;  
5. that the file copied by the script is really present in the destination directory.

Because the container running you may not have cron or the user instance of systemd actually *running*, the test harness will merely **read** the files you create.  
Therefore, focus on **authoring** all files exactly as requested, and on running the backup script manually once so that the log and the copied file really exist.

Infrastructure you must build:

A) Source material  
   • Directory: /home/user/backup_test/source  
   • File:      /home/user/backup_test/source/important.txt  
     Exact content (single line, NO trailing spaces):  
     Important data v1

B) Destination  
   • Directory: /home/user/backup_test/backup  
   • After your script runs once there must be an identical copy of important.txt here:  
     /home/user/backup_test/backup/important.txt

C) Backup script  
   • Path:  /home/user/backup_scripts/backup_once.sh  
   • Must be executable (chmod + x).  
   • Behaviour:  
     1. Copy /home/user/backup_test/source/important.txt to /home/user/backup_test/backup/important.txt (overwriting any older copy).  
     2. Append **one** log line to /home/user/backup_scripts/backup.log in the format  
        YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ss±hh:mm : Backed up important.txt  
        (That is the ISO-8601 date/time generated by “date --iso-seconds”, followed by literal text “ : Backed up important.txt”).  
     3. Produce no other output.  
   • You may implement the script any way you like as long as the two effects above are met.

D) Cron specification  
   • File: /home/user/backup_scripts/backup.cron  
   • **Single line** only (no leading/trailing blank lines!):  
     17 2 * * * /home/user/backup_scripts/backup_once.sh  
     This is “run every day at 02:17”.  
   • You do NOT have to install the entry with “crontab -e”; placing the file is enough for verification.

E) systemd user units  
   1. Service unit: ~/.config/systemd/user/backup_once.service  
      Exact text (including capitalisation and blank lines):  
      [Unit]  
      Description=One-off user backup

      [Service]  
      Type=oneshot  
      ExecStart=/home/user/backup_scripts/backup_once.sh  

   2. Timer unit:  ~/.config/systemd/user/backup_once.timer  
      Exact text:  
      [Unit]  
      Description=Run user backup 5 minutes after boot

      [Timer]  
      OnBootSec=5min  
      Unit=backup_once.service

      [Install]  
      WantedBy=default.target  

Important details to satisfy the grader:

• All five files and both directories must exist at the exact paths given above.  
• The shell script must have its executable bit set (-rwxr-xr-x or similar).  
• The log file (/home/user/backup_scripts/backup.log) must exist and contain **at least one** line that ends with “Backed up important.txt”.  
• The copy of important.txt must be present in /home/user/backup_test/backup and its contents must be byte-identical to the source file.  
• Line endings must be Unix “\n”.  
• Do **not** move or rename any of the required files/directories.  
• You do **not** need root; stay entirely in /home/user.

A minimal successful run therefore consists of roughly these steps (conceptually, not the literal commands):
1. Make the directories and source file.  
2. Write the backup script, make it executable.  
3. Execute the script once to generate the copy and add the log line.  
4. Author the cron file.  
5. Author the service and timer unit files.

Once all elements are in place, the automated grader will confirm the task by:
• reading the cron file and unit files verbatim;  
• checking executable permission bits on the script;  
• grepping the log file for “Backed up important.txt”;  
• comparing the two important.txt files with “cmp”.

No further console output is required beyond what you naturally emit while working.
truth:
Required final state for verification:

1. File system objects
   /home/user/backup_test/source                     (directory, any permissions ≥755)
   /home/user/backup_test/source/important.txt       (file, 1 line: "Important data v1\n")
   /home/user/backup_test/backup                     (directory, any permissions ≥755)
   /home/user/backup_test/backup/important.txt       (file, identical to source)
   /home/user/backup_scripts                         (directory, any permissions ≥755)
   /home/user/backup_scripts/backup_once.sh          (file, executable by user)
   /home/user/backup_scripts/backup.log              (file, at least 1 line, latest line must match regex "Backed up important.txt$")
   /home/user/backup_scripts/backup.cron             (file, single cron line exactly "17 2 * * * /home/user/backup_scripts/backup_once.sh\n")
   /home/user/.config/systemd/user                   (directory, any permissions ≥700)
   /home/user/.config/systemd/user/backup_once.service (file, exact 6-line body specified above, newline at end)
   /home/user/.config/systemd/user/backup_once.timer   (file, exact  nine-line body specified above, newline at end)

2. Permissions
   backup_once.sh must have at least 0755 (executable bit for user on).

3. Log content regex (grader uses grep -E):
   /home/user/backup_scripts/backup.log must contain at least one line matching:
   ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}([+-][0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}|Z) : Backed up important.txt$

4. important.txt copy identity:
   cmp --silent /home/user/backup_test/source/important.txt /home/user/backup_test/backup/important.txt must return 0.

No other files or directories are considered by the grader.
